title: What you were actually doing
blocks.1.text: You have written code for three lessons without me explaining what you were doing. That was deliberate. Now you have the experience, and the words will land on something.
blocks.2.text:
You know what things are called: `

` is the opening tag, `

` is the closing one, the content sits between them, and all three together are an element. One question is left that I kept walking past.
blocks.4.text: Go back and check. In none of the three lessons did you write a font, a size, a color or any spacing. You wrote two tags:
blocks.7.text: And still the heading came out big and bold, with blank space beneath it. Nobody asked for that. Where did it come from?
blocks.8.text: The browser brings its own stylesheet
blocks.9.text: Every browser loads a CSS file of its own before it ever sees yours. It is called the *user agent stylesheet*. It says what to do with each tag. Here is the real rule for `<h1>` in Chrome:
blocks.11.text: That is CSS. You did not write it — the people who built the browser did. `2em` means twice the normal size. The two margins are the blank space above and below that puzzled you.
blocks.12.text: Without that file your page would be one unbroken mush of identical text. Headings would not be big, lists would have no bullets, links would not be blue.
blocks.13.text: It is not the same everywhere
blocks.14.text: Chrome, Firefox and Safari carry different files. That is why the same HTML looks slightly different across browsers — a few pixels of margin here, a different font weight there.
blocks.15.text: You can see it yourself. Right-click a heading on any real site, then Inspect. In the styles panel scroll to the very bottom — it says User Agent Stylesheet, and these exact rules sit under it.
blocks.17.text: This stylesheet has the lowest weight of all. Write your own rule for `<h1>` and yours wins. So it is not a limit — it is the floor, not the ceiling.
blocks.18.text: And that is the point: if the look comes from a separate file that can be swapped, then the look was never part of the tag.
blocks.19.text: You did not pick `<h1>` because you wanted big text. You picked it because this is the heading. That it comes out big is someone else's decision — made by another person, in another file, and open to change.
blocks.21.text: HTML says what exists. CSS says how it looks. JavaScript says what it does. In that order — because you cannot color or animate something that is not there.
blocks.22.text: From now on, when you see something on your page you did not ask for — a size, a margin, a link color — your first thought already knows where to go.
blocks.24.items.0: The page looks styled because the browser ships its own CSS.
blocks.24.items.1: It is called the user agent stylesheet.
blocks.24.items.2: Big headings, margins, list bullets and blue links all come from there.
blocks.24.items.3: It differs slightly between browsers and loses to your own CSS.
blocks.24.items.4: HTML — what exists. CSS — how it looks. JavaScript — what it does.
